one_line_takeaway: A broad, well-organized taxonomy survey of RL-enhanced LLMs (RLHF, RLAIF, DPO and variants) with a challenges section touching safety alignment; it contributes structure and synthesis, not new methods or experiments, and every quantitative figure belongs to a cited primary source.
key_finding: As a survey it has findings-as-synthesis rather than a single empirical result: (1) RL post-training is now near-universal in frontier LLMs, splitting into reward-model-based RLHF/RLAIF (PPO) and reward-model-free preference optimization (DPO/ORPO/RPO/GRPO); (2) RLAIF (AI-generated feedback, LLM-as-reward-function, and self-rewarding) is an increasingly viable, more scalable substitute for human feedback; (3) the main open challenges for reward-model-based RL are reward-model out-of-distribution overconfidence/generalization, interpretability of scalar rewards (motivating multi-objective/distributional/representation reward models like ArmoRM, QRM, GPM), and safety (helpfulness-harmlessness trade-offs). All numeric evidence cited is drawn from the surveyed papers, not produced by this survey.
core_contribution: This is a literature SURVEY (v2/v3, updated Feb 24 2025), not an empirical or systems paper. Its own contribution is a structured taxonomy and consolidated review of reinforcement-learning-enhanced LLMs, organized into: (1) RL basics and how the RL pipeline (agent/environment/state/action/reward/policy) maps onto LLM fine-tuning; (2) a catalog of ~17 popular RL-enhanced LLMs (Table 1) grouped into traditional reward-model methods (RLHF/RLAIF with PPO) vs. simplified reward-model-free methods (DPO, RPO, ORPO, GRPO); (3) RLHF, split into human-feedback reward-model data collection and preference optimization; (4) RLAIF, split into distilling AI feedback for reward models, prompting LLMs as a reward function, and self-rewarding; (5) an analysis of RLHF/RLAIF challenges (out-of-distribution reward-model behavior, interpretability, and safety); and (6) DPO and its variants. The paper contributes organization, comparison, and challenge-identification, NOT any new algorithm, model, dataset, or experiment.
threat_model: N/A. This is a survey of alignment/capability techniques and defines no adversary, attack surface, or threat model of its own. It is security-ADJACENT in that Section 6.3 reviews safety-oriented alignment methods (Safe RLHF's helpfulness/harmlessness decoupling via a Lagrangian reward-vs-cost objective, Constitutional AI/RLAIF red-teaming, Quark toxicity unlearning, BeaverTails safety dataset, Rule-Based Rewards), but the paper frames these as alignment quality, not as defenses against a modeled attacker. No threat model was invented for this review.
limitations: Survey-level limitations: no empirical validation or independent verification of the metrics it reports (all numbers are second-hand from cited works and inherit their evaluation conditions); coverage is a fast-moving snapshot (Feb 2025), already partial by the time of reading; no unified benchmark makes the cross-method comparisons qualitative and non-commensurable; taxonomy boundaries (e.g., DPO variants vs. RLAIF self-rewarding) overlap; safety is treated as one of several alignment challenges rather than analyzed adversarially. A reader must not attribute any reported number to this paper's own experiments.

datasets_used: NOTE: the survey runs no experiments; these datasets are DISCUSSED/surveyed, not used by the authors
Skywork-Reward: 80,000 high-quality preference pairs filtered from an original 378,000
TULU-V2-mix: instruction-following mixture dataset (QA, code, translation, multi-turn)
UltraFeedback: 1M+ GPT-4 feedback annotations over 250,000 user-assistant interactions, from 60,000 instructions and 17 models
Magpie: 4,000,000 self-synthesized instruction-response pairs filtered to 300,000
OffsetBias: bias-mitigation preference dataset spanning six bias types
Nectar (Starling): 3,800,000 pairwise comparisons ranked by GPT-4
BeaverTails: 333,963 Q&A safety meta-label pairs and 361,903 expert helpfulness/harmlessness comparison pairs
quantitative_results: ALL figures below are reported BY the survey FROM cited primary works; the survey itself produced none
DeepSeek-R1-Zero: AIME 2024 pass@1 rose 15.6% -> 71.0%, and 86.7% with majority voting (matching OpenAI-o1-0912)
InstructGPT: the 1.3B model's outputs were preferred over 175B GPT-3 (100x fewer params)
Kimi-k1.5 long2short: up to 550% improvement over models such as GPT-4o and Claude 3.5
Starling-7B (RLAIF): MT-Bench 7.81 -> 8.09, AlpacaEval 88.51% -> 91.99%, Chatbot Arena ELO 1072 -> 1087
Eureka: outperformed human-designed rewards on 83% of tasks, average 52% performance improvement
Athene-70B: Arena-Hard-Auto 77.8% (vs GPT-4o 79.2%, Claude-3.5-Sonnet 79.3%), up from Llama-3-70B-Instruct's 46.6%
phi-3-mini: 69% MMLU and 8.38 MT-bench with only 3.8B params, trained on 3.3T tokens
Rule-Based Rewards (RBR): F1 of 97.1 vs 91.7 baseline (safety/helpfulness)
baselines_compared: Survey has no experimental baselines; the comparisons are conceptual between surveyed methods
Reward-model-based RLHF/RLAIF with PPO/TRPO vs. reward-model-free DPO/ORPO/RPO/GRPO
RLHF vs. RLAIF (human feedback vs. AI feedback for reward modeling)
DPO vs. RPO (RPO adds an implicit-reward term for the chosen-rejected quality gap that DPO ignores)
Eureka vs. Text2Reward vs. Self-Refined LLM (LLM-generated reward-code approaches)
Scalar reward models vs. interpretable variants: ArmoRM (multi-objective MoE), QRM (quantile/distributional), GPM (preference representation), Con-J (generative contrastive judge)
RBR vs. RLHF/RLAIF for integrating explicit safety rules
